lesson.menu_title: Habits, Hobbies & the Life You Want
lesson.menu_desc: Gerunds vs infinitives -- verb patterns -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 19 -- Habits, Hobbies & the Life You Want
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 19 -- Habits, Hobbies & the Life You Want

extra_audio.text: First, Pricila gave up jogging, because she just couldn't keep up with it. Then she decided to take up watercolour painting instead. Next, she dabbled in it for a few weeks, and slowly got the hang of it. After that, she found she was really into it, and started to make time for it every weekend. So now painting is the pastime that helps her unwind and broadens her horizons.

listenings.text: People always ask me what I do for fun, and the honest answer is that it keeps changing. For years I was a real creature of habit. I'd finish work, watch a bit of television, and go to bed, and I told myself I just didn't have time for hobbies. But a while ago I decided to make a change. I gave up scrolling on my phone every evening, because I couldn't stand wasting hours like that, and I took up watercolour painting instead. At first I was terrible. I kept making a mess, and I almost gave up after the first week. But I managed to find a little class on Saturday mornings, and slowly I got the hang of it. Now I'm completely hooked on it. Painting is the one thing that helps me unwind after a long day. I've also started to dabble in gardening, which I never thought I'd enjoy, and I'm planning to learn the piano next year. I can't afford to take expensive lessons, so I watch videos online. What I've learned is this: trying new things broadens your horizons, even at sixty. You don't stop being curious just because you get older. The life I want isn't about doing more. It's about making time for the things I'm genuinely into.

Hi Pricila, it's me, returning your message about finding more balance. You asked how I manage to fit hobbies around such a busy job, so here are a few honest thoughts. The first thing I had to admit was that I'm not very good at switching off. I used to keep working in the evenings and avoid taking real breaks, and I told myself I'd relax later. But later never came. So I started small. I decided to stop checking my email after seven, and I made a rule to spend at least one evening a week doing something just for me. For me that's cooking. It's an acquired taste, I know, but chopping vegetables and following a recipe really helps me unwind. The big lesson was about work-life balance. You can't pour from an empty cup, as they say. If you never make time for the things you enjoy, you give up a part of yourself. So my advice is simple: pick one thing you're curious about, and just dabble in it. Don't worry about being good at it. The point is to keep growing and to remember to enjoy your own life. Talk soon, and let me know what you decide to take up.

paras: Ask people what they do, and most will tell you about their work. Ask them what they enjoy doing, and you often get a pause. Somewhere between the job and the to-do list, the hobbies quietly disappear. We give up the guitar, we stop going to the pool, we keep meaning to take up that language -- and the years go by. The life we want stays a plan we never quite start. The grammar of all this is small but stubborn: we enjoy doing things, we avoid doing things, we decide to do things, we hope to do things. Get the patterns right, and you can finally say what you want out loud.

The happiest people are rarely the busiest. They are the ones who have learned to make time for what they love, and who are not afraid to be beginners again. Taking up a new pastime at fifty or sixty is not a waste of time; it is one of the surest ways to broaden your horizons. You dabble in painting, you get the hang of the piano, you discover you are hopelessly into birdwatching. None of it has to be useful. The point is that doing something for its own sake -- something you can't stand to give up -- keeps you curious, and a curious mind stays young.

There is a quiet honesty in admitting what you are really into. Some pleasures are an acquired taste; others hook you at once. Some habits you keep, some you give up, and the trick is knowing which is which. A creature of habit is not a prisoner -- routines can be the floor you build a life on -- but every now and then it helps to try something new, just to remember that you still can. The life you want is not waiting at the end of a long list of tasks. It is made, one unhurried afternoon at a time, of the things you choose to keep doing.

gist.prompt: What is the writer's main point about hobbies and the life you want?
choices: You should give up all your hobbies once you are older, because they are a waste of useful time.
The life you want is built, one unhurried afternoon at a time, by making time for what you are genuinely into and not being afraid to try new things.
The busiest people are always the happiest, because they never stop working on their to-do list.

items: The writer says that when you ask people what they enjoy doing, you often get a pause.

None of it has to be useful. Doing something for its own sake keeps you curious.

matching.title: Match each expression to its meaning
defs: To start a new hobby or activity
To stop doing something, often a habit
To really like and be interested in something
To learn how to do something with practice
To relax and let go of stress
To try something casually, without going deep
Something you only learn to like after a while
To gain new experiences and a wider view of life

rephrase.title: Choose -ing or to + verb
items: I really enjoy ... (paint) in the mornings.
I really enjoy painting in the mornings.
She decided ... (take up) the piano this year.
She decided to take up the piano this year.
He keeps ... (put off) his Saturday class.
He keeps putting off his Saturday class.
We can't afford ... (waste) our weekends.
We can't afford to waste our weekends.
I stopped ... (scroll) on my phone, and now I read instead. (= I no longer scroll)
I stopped scrolling on my phone, and now I read instead.
Remember ... (make) time for what you love. (= don't forget to do it)
Remember to make time for what you love.

answerkey.title: Reveal the verb-pattern answer key
list: I really enjoy painting in the mornings.
She decided to take up the piano this year.
He keeps putting off his Saturday class.
We can't afford to waste our weekends.
I stopped scrolling on my phone, and now I read instead.
Remember to make time for what you love.
answerkey.note: Verb patterns: some verbs are followed by the -ing form (enjoy / avoid / keep / give up / can't stand + doing); others are followed by to + base verb (decide / manage / hope / plan / afford / remember-to + to do). A few verbs change meaning: stop doing = quit the activity, but stop to do = pause in order to do something; remember doing = recall a past action, but remember to do = not forget a future one; try doing = experiment, but try to do = make an effort. After a preposition we ALWAYS use -ing (good at planning, interested in learning). And a gerund can be the subject: Travelling broadens the mind.

analyse.title: Close reading
items: The writer says 'a creature of habit is not a prisoner'. What does the writer mean -- when are routines good, and when is it worth trying something new?
The text says doing something 'for its own sake' keeps you curious, and 'a curious mind stays young'. Do you agree that a hobby does not have to be useful to be worth your time? Why?
The writer ends that the life you want is 'made, one unhurried afternoon at a time, of the things you choose to keep doing'. What is one thing you would choose to keep doing -- or take up?
